Unaligned Data Access

Detailed Description

Routines for helping with accessing unaligned memory.

Data Structures

struct  vec_UnalignedPointer
 An object corresponding to an unaligned pointer. More...
struct  vec_UnalignedArray
 An object corresponding to an unaligned array of uint64_t. More...
struct  _vec_UnalignedStruct
 An internal type used for the implementation of vec_unaligned_load(). More...

Defines

#define vec_init_unaligned_pointer(result, start_ptr)
#define vec_init_unaligned_array(array, start_ptr)
#define vec_unaligned_array_load_last(array)
#define vec_unaligned_array_load_next(array)
#define vec_unaligned_load(ptr)
#define vec_unaligned_load_1(out0, uptr)
#define vec_unaligned_load_2(out0, out1, uptr)
#define vec_unaligned_load_4(out0, out1, out2, out3,uptr)

Define Documentation

#define vec_init_unaligned_pointer ( result,
start_ptr   ) 

Initializes vec_UnalignedPointer 'result' to point to 'start_ptr', a possibly unaligned pointer.

#define vec_init_unaligned_array ( array,
start_ptr   ) 

Declares and initializes an unaligned array, so you can stride through it with vec_unaligned_array_load_next.

Note that, because of the way this is pipelined, this may end up reading one word past the end of your array. So make sure there is valid memory there.

Examples:
Unaligned_Data_Access.

#define vec_unaligned_array_load_last ( array   ) 

Returns the last unsigned integer from an unaligned array.

It does not advance the pointer to the next value or load any values. You do not need to use this to read the last array element, but it is a little cleaner to do so if you already know a specific load will be the last one.

#define vec_unaligned_array_load_next ( array   ) 

Returns the next unsigned integer from an unaligned array and advances the pointer to the next value.

Note that, because of the way this is pipelined, this may end up reading one word past the end of your array. So make sure there is valid memory there.

Examples:
Unaligned_Data_Access.

#define vec_unaligned_load ( ptr   ) 

Loads an unaligned long from an arbitrary address.

This is the most efficient way to load one unaligned value. If you are going to load several values with the same alignment, consider using vec_init_unaligned_pointer() and vec_unaligned_load_[124], or for an array use vec_init_unaligned_array() and vec_unaligned_array_load_next().

Examples:
Unaligned_Data_Access.

#define vec_unaligned_load_1 ( out0,
uptr   ) 

Loads one uint64_t from the given vec_UnalignedPointer and stores it in out0.

#define vec_unaligned_load_2 ( out0,
out1,
uptr   ) 

Loads two uint64_t values from the given vec_UnalignedPointer and stores them in out0 and out1.

#define vec_unaligned_load_4 ( out0,
out1,
out2,
out3,
uptr   ) 

Loads four uint64_t values from the given vec_UnalignedPointer and stores them in out0, out1, out2 and out3.
